locked_facts: Static friction is self-adjusting: 0 ≤ f_s ≤ μ_s·N
Maximum static friction = μ_s·N (not always equal to μ_s·N)
Kinetic friction = μ_k·N (constant once sliding begins)
μ_k < μ_s always — kinetic friction is less than maximum static
Friction acts parallel to surface, opposing relative motion or tendency
Normal force N = mg on horizontal surface
truth_statements: Static friction is a self-adjusting force that varies from zero to a maximum value of μ_s times the normal force
The maximum value of static friction is μ_s N, which occurs just before the body begins to slide
Kinetic friction is less than the maximum static friction
Friction opposes the relative motion or the tendency of relative motion between surfaces
The coefficient of kinetic friction is always less than the coefficient of static friction

aha_moment.what_to_show: Applied force bar grows slowly and friction bar matches it exactly — then at the critical point (F = μ_s·N), the block suddenly starts sliding and friction bar DROPS to the lower μ_k·N level
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The sudden snap DOWN of the friction bar from μ_s·N to μ_k·N the instant sliding begins — this discontinuous drop is the aha
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that friction is NOT always μN — it matches the applied force until the maximum, then drops to a lower constant value

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Block on rough surface. Applied force F = 0. No motion. Friction force = 0. Weight and normal force arrows shown in balance.
simulation_states.emphasis: When no force is applied, friction is zero — not μN

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Applied force F increases gradually. Friction bar grows to match F exactly. Block remains stationary. A dashed line at μ_s·N shows the limit.
simulation_states.emphasis: Friction = applied force (self-adjusting). Block does not move. Friction has not yet reached its maximum.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: F reaches μ_s·N — block starts sliding. Friction bar instantly drops to μ_k·N (lower value). Block accelerates. The drop is visually dramatic.
simulation_states.emphasis: The sudden DROP in friction from μ_s·N to μ_k·N is the key insight. Kinetic friction < maximum static friction.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust applied force and mass. See friction bar respond. Below μ_s·N: friction matches F. Above: friction locks at μ_k·N and block slides.
simulation_states.emphasis: Slowly increase F and watch friction match it. Cross the threshold and see the discontinuous drop to kinetic friction.

common_misconceptions.belief: Friction always equals μN — it is a fixed value
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says static friction is self-adjusting from zero up to a maximum of μ_s·N. Only kinetic friction is a fixed value μ_k·N.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show friction bar growing with applied force (self-adjusting) then snapping down to μ_k·N when sliding starts

common_traps: using wrong normal force on incline
